coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode sensor for visual detection of malathion in cabbage based on carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Nini
Hu, Xuetao
Li, Wenting
Mwakosya, Anjelina W.
Guo, Ziang
Xu, Yiwei
Huang, Xiaowei
Li, Zhihua
Zhang, Xinai
Zou, Xiaobo
Shi, Jiyong
coredata.dc:description: A dual-mode fluorescence/colorimetric sensor based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (GNPs) was developed for visual detection of malathion in cabbage. The CQDs-GNPs nanocomposite exhibited emission wavelength at 527 nm and absorption wavelength at 524 nm. The fluorescence intensity increased and absorption decreased with addition of malathion. Fluorescence and colorimetric calibration curves were established based on fluorescence intensity (R2 = 0.9914) and absorbance (R2 = 0.9608) in the range of 1 × 10−9–1 × 10−2 M, respectively. Furthermore, fluorescence and colorimetric standard arrays were prepared for visual detection of malathion according to the change of fluorescence brightness and color. Finally, the approximate concentrations of malathion in cabbage samples were estimated by the standard arrays and naked eyes. The calibration curves were used for accurate detection in cabbage samples with recoveries of 89.9%–103.4% (fluorescence) and 88.7%–107.6% (colorimetric). The established sensor for visual malathion detection in cabbage was accurate with strong application potential, especially for rapid screening.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs-GNPs
Malathion
Fluorescence
Colorimetric
Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of standard arrays combined with a smartphone
Detection of malathion in cabbage by the CQDs-GNPs
Development of an CQDs-GNPs based method
Establishment of calibration curves based on CQDs-GNPs
Detection of malathion in cabbage by the CQDs-GNPs-based sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (grant numbers 2018YFD0400800); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 31772073, 31671844); the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant numbers BE2019359); Distinguished Professor Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant number 202074); Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CQDs; (b) TEM image of GNPs; (c) Fluorescent spectra of CQDs and CQDs-GNPs under 420 nm excitation, inset: photographs of the CQDs (left) and CQDs-GNPs (right) solution under UV light of 365 nm; (d) Absorbance spectra of CQDs-GNPs and CQDs-GNPs-NaCl, inset: photographs of the CQDs-GNPs (left) and CQDs-GNPs-NaCl (right) solution under natural light.
Principle diagram for malathion detection based on CQDs and GNPs.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of CQDs-GNPs in the presence of different concentrations of malathion; (b) the linear plot of fluorescence intensities at 527 nm versus different malathion concentrations from 0 to 1 × 10−2 M; (c) UV spectra of CQDs-GNPs in the presence of different concentrations of malathion; (d) the linear plot of absorbance at 524 nm versus different malathion concentrations from 0 to 1 × 10−2 M.
Standard arrays of fluorescence (upper) and colorimetric (bottom).
Visual detection of malathion in cabbage samples.
Detection results and recoveries of malathion in cabbages.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode sensor for visual detection of malathion in cabbage based on carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Xuefu Road No. 301, Jingkou District, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-mode fluorescence/colorimetric sensor based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (GNPs) was developed for visual detection of malathion in cabbage. The CQDs-GNPs nanocomposite exhibited emission wavelength at 527 nm and absorption wavelength at 524 nm. The fluorescence intensity increased and absorption decreased with addition of malathion. Fluorescence and colorimetric calibration curves were established based on fluorescence intensity (R2 = 0.9914) and absorbance (R2 = 0.9608) in the range of 1 × 10−9–1 × 10−2 M, respectively. Furthermore, fluorescence and colorimetric standard arrays were prepared for visual detection of malathion according to the change of fluorescence brightness and color. Finally, the approximate concentrations of malathion in cabbage samples were estimated by the standard arrays and naked eyes. The calibration curves were used for accurate detection in cabbage samples with recoveries of 89.9%–103.4% (fluorescence) and 88.7%–107.6% (colorimetric). The established sensor for visual malathion detection in cabbage was accurate with strong application potential, especially for rapid screening.

ce:section.ce:para: 1,2-bis (ethoxycarbonyl) ethyl O,O-dimethyl phosphorodithioate, commonly known as malathion, is a non-systemic broad-spectrum organophosphorus insecticide (Alavinia et al., 2019; Kalender, Uzun, Durak, Demir & Kalender, 2010). It is widely used to protect tea, vegetables, rice and tobacco from insects (e.g., piercing-sucking mouthparts and chewing mouthparts) and reduce the losses of agricultural production (Sun, Liu, Dang, Li, Zhou & Wang, 2012). Currently, approximately 700 malathion crops are registered and about 80,000 tons malathion are used annually in China (Liu et al., 2020). Recent reports have demonstrated that malathion can cause teratogenic, carcinogenic, mutagenic, endocrine disruption, neurotoxicity and other hazards to human body after being absorbed (Selmi, Rtibi, Grami, Sebai & Marzouki, 2018). Cabbage is a widely consumed vegetable crop in the world (Lee et al., 2014; Mi, Liu, Xie, Zhou, Wu & Dai, 2019). Thus, malathion detection in cabbage is necessary to minimize consumer side effects while consuming cabbage.

Currently, various methods such as gas chromatography (GC) (Lofty, Abd El-Aleem & Monir, 2013), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Abu-Qare & Abou-Donia, 2001), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (García-Ruiz, Álvarez-Llamas, Puerta, Blanco, Sanz-Medel & Marina, 2005), high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) (Timofeeva, Shishov, Kanashina, Dzema & Bulatov, 2017) and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (Su et al., 2011) have been developed for malathion detection. Although these methods are highly accurate, they still have some disadvantages, such as high cost, complex pre-processing and high requirements for instruments and operators. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a simple, rapid, low-cost, and sensitive method for malathion detection.

Nanosensor techniques possess obvious advantages of effective, high sensitivity, fast response and simple pretreatment for malathion detection (Bala et al., 2017; Qiu et al., 2019). In recent years, various sensors have been developed for malathion detection such as (3-glycidyloxypropyl) trimethoxysilane (GOPS) and dimethyloctadecyl [3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ammoniumchloride (DMOAP) (Kim Hong & Jang, 2020), hollow carbon spheres coated with needle-like polyaniline (HCS@PANI) (He et al., 2018), palladium-gold bimetallic nanozyme (Pd@AuNRs) (Singh, Tripathi, Kumar & Nara, 2017), polyaniline nanofibers/single wall carbon nanotubes (PAIN-ES/SWCN) (Ebrahim, El-Raey, Hefnawy, Ibrahim, Soliman & Abdel-Fattah, 2014). Despite the importance of these methods, they still have some deficiencies. On the one hand, some of them suffered from disadvantages of complex synthesis and modification of nanomaterials, long detection time and high cost. On the other hand, most of nanosensors were single mode assays, offered only one kind of output signal, and they were less sensitive and accurate comparing with multi-mode sensors. In summary, developing a new dual-mode nanosensor with quickness, sensitivity and selectivity for visual detection of malathion is highly desired. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a fluorescent nanomaterial have attracted more and more attention due to their excellent optical, physical and chemical properties (eg., strong fluorescence, biocompatibility and chemical stability) (Hu et al., 2019). Gold nanoparticles (GNPs) were widely used in colorimetric nanosensors because the color of GNPs solution would vary significantly with the distance of GNPs changed and can be easily distinguished by naked eye without any assistance of precise instruments (Lee, Gaston, Weiss & Zhang, 2013). Consequently, CQDs have been used in fluorescent sensors and GNPs have been used in colorimetric sensors to detect antibiotics (Khavani, Izadyar & Housaindokht, 2019), proteins (Eissa et al., 2018), heavy metal ions (Luo, Xie, Meng, Sun, Ding & Zhou, 2019), organics (Park, Kang, Baek, You, Jang & Na, 2019) and pesticides (Bala, Kumar, Bansal, Sharma & Wangoo, 2016). Therefore, CQDs-GNPs based sensor would possess dual-mode response of fluorescence and colorimetric, and have a great application prospect for visual detection.

In the present study, a dual-mode visual method with fluorescence and colorimetric response was developed for rapid and accurate detection of malathion in cabbage. In the presence of malathion, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-GNPs increased and the absorbance decreased. Besides, the fluorescence brightness was enhanced and the color changed from red to blue. Based on that, the calibration curves and standard arrays of fluorescence and colorimetric were prepared and used for malathion detection in cabbage samples. This proposed dual-mode method was not only sensitive and selective for accurate detection of malathion, but also convenient to observed the fluorescence brightness and color change of the sensor with naked eyes, allowing for visual detection of malathion.

Polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG 200) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O), tri-sodium citrate, sucrose, sodium chloride (NaCl), Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4), phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH = 7.4) and acetone were purchased from Sinopeia Chemical Reagent Co., LTD (Shanghai, China). Malathion, profenofos, isocarbophos, dimethoate, dichlorvos and parathion were procured from China Standard Materials Network. The malathion aptamer sequence is 5′-ATC CGT CAC ACC TGC TCT TAT ACA CAA TTG TTT TTC TCT TAA CTT CTT GAC TGC TGG TGT TGG CTC CCG TAT-3′ which is synthesized by Shanghai Sangon Biological Science & Technology Company (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without any purification. The ultrapure water used in the experiments was obtained from Milli-Q, Millipore filter system (Millipore Co., USA) and had a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm−1.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of CQDs, GNPs were obtained from JEOL JEM-2100 (Japan) at 200 kV maximum accelerating voltage. UV spectra of GNPs were recorded by UV-1601 UV spectrophotometer (Beifen-Ruili Analytical Instrument Co. Ltd, China). The fluorescent spectrum of CQDs and CQDs-GNPs were determined by Crad-F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Leng Guang technology Co. Ltd, China).

A smartphone-based visualization device was developed and used for visual detection of malathion. The device consists of light box and a smartphone (Fig. S1). The inner wall of the light box is pasted with black or white paper. The light box with black inner wall is used to obtain fluorescence images. The light box with white inner wall is used to obtain colorimetric images. Light source includes natural light and 365 nm UV light with power of 12 W (Shanghai Anting Scientific Instrumental Factory, China). The light is fixed at the top of light box and parallel to the well plate. The smartphone is used to acquire and display fluorescent and colorimetric images. The camera of smartphone is hold in the front of the light box at a 45-degree angle to acquire images. The procedure of obtaining images is as follows: (1) Pasting black or white paper in to inner wall of light box; (2) Fixing light source at the top of light box; (3) Placing solution on the object stage and turning on the light; (4) Taking photographs of the solution by the camera in smartphone.

CQDs were synthesized according to the citrate reduction pro-cedures with a minor modification (Hu et al., 2019). 1 mL sucrose solution (30%) and 200 μL of concentrated H2SO4 were put in a 10 mL beaker, then 6 mL PEG was added into the beaker and mixed. The mixture was heated for 60 s in microwave oven (Midea, China) with a power of 900 W, which resulted to the successful preparation of carbon quantum dot when the solution changed from colorless to golden yellow. The obtained quantum dot solution was dialyzed in ultra-pure water for 24 h with dialysis tube (MWCO = 1000, Thermo Fisher, USA). Then, NaOH solution was used to adjust pH to 7. The resultant CQDs were diluted to 100 mL and stored at 4℃ in refrigerator for further characterization and utilization. The device for synthesizing CQDs was shown in Scheme S1.

The GNPs were synthesized by citrate reduction of chloroauric acid (Bala et al., 2016). Firstly, 100 mL 1 mmol/L HAuCl4 was added to 250 mL flask containing reflux device and heated to boiling followed by the addition of 1% tri-sodium citrate (10 mL). Then the color of the solution turned pale yellow to wine red, indicating the formation of GNPs. Afterwards, the solution was heated for another 10 min and allowed to cool to room temperature by stirring. Finally, the resultant GNPs was diluted to 500 mL and stored in dark bottles at 4℃. Scheme S2 shown the device for synthesizing GNPs.

Malathion solutions with concentration of 0, 1 × 10−9, 1 × 10−8, 1 × 10−7, 1 × 10−6, 1 × 10−5, 1 × 10−4, 1 × 10−3 and 1 × 10−2 M were selected to establish calibration curves. Several parameters (reaction time and concentration of NaCl, aptamers) were optimized. 200 μL CQDs solution was mixed with 200 μL GNPs, 20 μL aptamers (5 μM), 20 μL malathion of different concentration respectively and incubated for 2 min. 50 μL NaCl (1.8 M) were added to above mixtures and incubated for 12 min. Fluorescent spectra were obtained by a fluorescence spectrometer to establish a fluorescent calibration curve. Ultraviolet visible (UV) absorption spectra were obtained by a UV spectrophotometer to establish a colorimetric calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of standard arrays combined with a smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence and colorimetric standard arrays were prepared by 96-well plate with a transparent bottom and a smartphone. CQDs, GNPs, malathion solution, aptamers and NaCl solution were added into centrifuge tube according to the procedure described in section 2.3.1. The mixed solution (500 μL) was removed from centrifuge tube to wells respectively. Photographs of 96-well plates were obtained by the smartphone according to the procedures in Section 2.1. The standard arrays were consisted of images with 0, 1 × 10−9, 1 × 10−8, 1 × 10−7, 1 × 10−6, 1 × 10−5, 1 × 10−4, 1 × 10−3 and 1 × 10−2 M of malathion. The fluorescent brightness and color of standard images was evaluated by naked eye to predict malathion concentration.

The fluorescent and UV responses of CQDs-GNPs in the presence of malathion or common compounds in cabbage (profenofos, isocarbophos, dimethoate, dichlorvos, parathion) were recorded to evaluate the selectivity. All experiments were performed in triplicate on different days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of malathion in cabbage by the CQDs-GNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs-GNPs based method was used for detecting cabbage adulterated with malathion in order to evaluate its practical application. Cabbage samples were purchased from Kaiyuan supermarket, Zhenjiang, China. Each cabbage sample was pretreated as follows: cabbage was juiced by juicer. Then, the obtained liquid was transferred to a beaker and ultrasonic treated for 5 min to make it disperse evenly. Afterwards, the above solution (5 g) was mixed with malathion standard solution of different concentration (1 mL, 0, 1 × 10−7, 1 × 10−4, 1 × 10−2 M) and diluted into 50 mL with acetone. After 15 min ultrasonic treatment, the mixture was separated by 0.22 μm filter membrane and the filtrate was collected. The collection was concentrated at 60℃ for 30 min. Finally, samples were diluted into 10 mL with a PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) and the concentrations of malathion in cabbage samples were 0, 1 × 10−8, 1 × 10−5, 1 × 10−3 M, respectively. Cabbage samples adulterated by malathion were detected according to the method described in section 2.3.

The accurate malathion concentration was detected by calibration curves according the method described in section 2.3.1. 200 μL CQDs, 200 μL GNPs, 20 μL of cabbage samples (n = 3), 20 μL aptamers (5 μM) and 50 μL NaCl (1.8 M) were mixed in cuvettes for further quantitative investigation. Fluorescent spectra and UV absorption spectra were obtained by fluorescence spectrometer and UV spectrophotometer, respectively. The malathion concentration of cabbage samples was calculated via fluorescent and colorimetric calibration curves.

The approximate malathion concentration was detected by fluorescent and colorimetric standard arrays according to the method described in section 2.3.2. 200 μL CQDs, 200 μL GNPs, 20 μL of cabbage samples (n = 3), 20 μL aptamers (5 μM) and 50 μL NaCl (1.8 M) were mixed completely and added into the wells of a 96-well plate. Photographs of cabbage samples were obtained by the smartphone. The malathion concentration of cabbage samples was detected by comparing fluorescent brightness and color of standard images and cabbage samples’ images.

The average fluorescent spectra of CQDs under different excitation wavelengths (400, 410, 420, 430, 440 and 450 nm) were recorded in Fig. S2. The obtained fluorescence spectrum indicated that CQDs have the highest fluorescence intensity at 527 nm under the excitation wavelength at 420 nm. Consequently, CQDs were synthesized with the optimal excitation wavelength of 420 nm and maximum emission wavelength of 527 nm. The synthesized CQDs solution was yellow (Fig. S2b, left) under the natural light and possessed a green fluorescence under a UV light at 365 nm (Fig. S2b, right). TEM image of CQDs showed that CQDs possessed near-spherical morphology with a diameter of about 5.0 nm (Fig. 1 a). TEM image of GNPs revealed that the synthesized GNPs were near-spherical with a particle size of about 20–30 nm and were dispersed in aqueous solution (Fig. 1b). Fluorescence intensity and brightness of CQDs and CQDs-GNPs were compared in Fig. 1c. Fluorescence intensity of CQDs-GNPs was much lower than that of CQDs under 420 nm excitation, and fluorescence brightness of CQDs-GNPs was lower than that of CQDs under UV light excitation. The fluorescence of CQDs was quenched after the addition of GNPs, that was attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CQDs and GNPs (Rodzik-Czałka et al., 2018). CQDs-GNPs compound had an absorbance peak at 524 nm, but absorbance at 524 nm decreased and appeared a new absorbance peak with the addition of NaCl (Fig. 1d). Besides, the color of CQDs-GNPs and CQDs-GNPs-NaCl solution was red and blue, respectively (Fig. 1d, inner). The sensors’ change of absorbance spectra and color was cause by the aggregation of GNPs in NaCl solution (Řezanka, Řezanková, Matějka & Král, 2010). Evidently, the results of Fig. 1 confirmed the successful fabrication of CQDs and GNPs required for nanosensor assembly and the feasibility of the CQDs-GNPs-based system for dual-mode visual sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of an CQDs-GNPs based method
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CQDs and GNPs were used for the detection of malathion according to mechanism demonstrated in Fig. 2 . CQDs emitted fluorescence under excitation, but the fluorescence of CQDs would be quenched when GNPs adsorbed on the surface of CQDs (Fig. 2a). The fluorescence of CQDs would recovery due to the aggregation of GNPs in NaCl solution (Fig. 2b). However, in the presence of aptamers, GNPs were protected by the aptamers form aggregation, resulting in the fluorescence of CQDs were quenched again. When malathion was added, the aptamer combined with malathion and GNPs aggregated due to the loss of aptamers on their surface, concluding in the recovery of the fluorescence of CQDs (Fig. 2c). Besides, the color of GNPs would vary significantly with the distance of GNPs changed. As the aggregation of GNPs, the color of the GNPs solution changes from the original red to blue.

In the process of constructing sensor, several parameters (reaction time and amount of NaCl, aptamers) were optimized to achieve the highest sensitivity (Fig. S3). 200 μL CQDs, 200 μL GNPs, 20 μL aptamers (5 μM), 50 μL NaCl (1.8 M) and 12 min reaction time were chosen as the optimum conditions. Calibration curves were developed by the fluorescence spectrometer and UV spectrophotometer, and standard arrays were developed by smartphone for malathion detection under optimum conditions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of calibration curves based on CQDs-GNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Calibration curves of fluorescence and colorimetric were established by the fluorescence spectrometer and UV spectrophotometer, respectively (Fig. 3 ). The fluorescent intensity at 527 nm of established sensor increased gradually when the malathion concentration was raised from 0 to 1 × 10−2 M (Fig. 3a). A good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9914) was obtained between fluorescence intensities at 527 nm and the logarithm of malathion concentrations in the range of 1 × 10−9 M to 1 × 10−2 M (Fig. 3b). The regression equation was y = 70.791 log10x + 817.33, where × was the concentration of malathion, the unit of × was M, and y was the fluorescent intensity at 527 nm. As show in Fig. 3c and Fig. 3d, absorbance at 524 nm of CQDs-GNPs-based sensor linearly decreased with the increase of malathion concentration in the range of 1 × 10−9 M to 1 × 10−2 M, with the regression equation y = -0.0145 log10x + 0.3064 (R2 = 0.9608), where × was the concentration of malathion, the unit of × was M, and y was the absorbance at 524 nm. Finally, LOD was estimated to be 0.13 × 10−9 M (fluorescence, n = 11) and 0.59 × 10−9 M (colorimetric, n = 11) according to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC).

Standard arrays of fluorescence and colorimetric were prepared by 96-well plate and a smartphone. Compared with hulking instruments, smartphone was more portable to collect and process images used for preparation of standard arrays. Besides, smartphone was convenient to carry and take photos of sensors on the spot to compare with standard arrays. The standard arrays were consisted of images with 0, 1 × 10−9, 1 × 10−8, 1 × 10−7, 1 × 10−6, 1 × 10−5, 1 × 10−4, 1 × 10−3 and 1 × 10−2 M concentration of malathion (Fig. 4 ). With the increase of malathion concentration, the fluorescent brightness gradually increased and the color of solution changed from red to blue. The fluorescence brightness was related to the fluorescence intensity, the fluorescence brightness of CQDs-GNPs increased with higher fluorescence intensity. The color of the CQDs-GNPs solution was related to absorbance of 524 nm and wavelength of absorption peak. With the decrease of absorbance of 524 nm and the emergence of a new absorption peak, the color of the solution gradually changed from original red to purple and even blue. Thus, standard arrays of fluorescence and colorimetric can be used for visual detection of malathion.

The sensitivity of the present method was compared with previous reports was list in Table S1. The established sensor was sensitive and had broad liner range. Additionally, compared with the single signal sensors, the dual-mode sensor reduced the interference of background on the malathion detection and made the detection results more robust. Furthermore, the present method realized the visual detection of malathion, which was beneficial to the rapid and on-side detection of malathion.

The fluorescent and colorimetric responses of CQDs-GNPs in the presence of malathion against common compounds in cabbage (profenofos, isocarbophos, dimethoate, dichlorvos, parathion) were recorded to evaluate the selectivity of the method (Fig. S4). The concentration of malathion was 1 × 10−3 M and the concentration of all these interferences was 1 M. In the presence of malathion, fluorescence intensities of sensor were significantly recovered and absorbance was weakened. Additionally, fluorescence brightness and color of sensor changed a lot in the presence of malathion. Whereas, these common compounds showed weak influence towards CQDs-GNPs system. Even though the concentration of interference was much higher than that of malathion, fluorescence intensities, absorbance, fluorescence brightness and color of CQDs-GNPs changed slightly in the presence of interferences. These results implied that the established sensor can be used for selective detection of malathion in cabbage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of malathion in cabbage by the CQDs-GNPs-based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Four types of cabbage samples (Cabbage 1, Cabbage 2, Cabbage 3 and Cabbage 4) with malathion concentrations of 0, 1 × 10−8, 1 × 10−5 and 1 × 10−3 M were measured by CQDs-GNPs-.

The accurate malathion concentrations of Cabbage 1, Cabbage 2, Cabbage 3 and Cabbage 4 detected by calibration curve of fluorescence were 1.82 ± 0.28 × 10−9, 0.90 ± 0.15 × 10−8, 0.91 ± 0.12 × 10−5 and 1.03 ± 0.14 × 10−3 M (average founded ± standard deviation), respectively (Table 1 ). The accurate malathion concentrations of Cabbage 1, Cabbage 2, Cabbage 3 and Cabbage 4 detected by calibration curve of UV were 5.69 ± 0.76 × 10−10, 0.89 ± 0.12 × 10−8, 1.07 ± 0.08 × 10−5 and 0.97 ± 0.16 × 10−3 M (average founded ± standard deviation) (Table 1). The fluorescence intensity at 527 nm and absorbance at 524 nm of samples was obtained by fluorescence spectrometer and UV spectrophotometer, respectively. Then, they were severally substituted into the regression equation [y = 70.791 log10x + 817.33] (fluorescence) and [y = -0.0145 log10x + 0.3064] (UV) to calculate the concentration of malathion in samples. The method for malathion detection in cabbage described in section 2.4 was simple and took less time that was 30 min. The performance of detecting malathion in Cabbage 1, Cabbage 2, Cabbage 3 and Cabbage 4 indicated that the CQDs-GNPs-based fluorescent and UV calibration curves are feasible for detecting malathion in cabbage accurately with assistance of fluorescence and UV spectrometers.

The approximate malathion concentration of Cabbage 1, Cabbage 2, Cabbage 3 and Cabbage 4 were detected by the fluorescent arrays was 0, 1 × 10−8, 1 × 10−5 and 1 × 10−3 M, respectively (Fig. 5 ). The fluorescent and colorimetric images of cabbage samples were obtained via smartphone and compared with arrays by naked eyes. The fluorescence images of Cabbage 1, Cabbage 2, Cabbage 3 and Cabbage 4 were similar with the fluorescence standard images of 0, 1 × 10−8, 1 × 10−5 and 1 × 10−3 M, the colorimetric images were similar with the colorimetric standard images of 0, 1 × 10−8, 1 × 10−5 and 1 × 10−3 M as shown in Fig. 5, respectively. These results indicated that fluorescence and colorimetric standard arrays can be used for visual detection of malathion in cabbage by comparing the images of samples and standard arrays. The detection of malathion in cabbage is rapid with a less time that is less than 15 min according to the method described in section 2.4. In conclusion, fluorescence and colorimetric standard arrays can be used for the rapid and simple detection of cabbage adulterated with malathion.

A dual-mode sensor based on CQDs-GNPs was developed for visual detection of malathion in cabbage. Both the calibration curves and standard arrays were established for fluorescence and colorimetric detection of malathion in the range of 1 × 10−9 M – 1 × 10−2 M. According to the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity at 527 nm, absorption at 524 nm and the concentration of malathion, calibration curves of fluorescence (R2 = 0.9914) and colorimetric (R2 = 0.9608) were established for malathion detection. Besides, standard arrays of fluorescence and colorimetric were prepared for visual detection of malathion in accordance to the change of fluorescence brightness and color. Finally, the developed standard arrays and calibration curves were applied to the detection of malathion in cabbage samples. The recoveries of calibration curves detection were 89.9% – 103.4% (fluorescence) and 88.7% – 107.6% (colorimetric), and the malathion concentration can be obtained by the comparing of imagines of samples and standard arrays. This biosensor achieved visual and accurate analysis of malathion in cabbage with less time and light instruments. The developed method may pave a new way for rapid, sensitive and selective detection of malathion in cabbage.

Nini Liang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xuetao Hu: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Wenting Li: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Anjelina W. Mwakosya: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Ziang Guo: Validation, Investigation. Yiwei Xu: Formal analysis. Xiaowei Huang: Data curation. Zhihua Li: Software, Visualization. Xinai Zhang: Formal analysis. Xiaobo Zou: Supervision. Jiyong Shi: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (grant numbers 2018YFD0400800); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 31772073, 31671844); the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant numbers BE2019359); Distinguished Professor Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant number 202074); Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.128494.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Abu-Qare and Abou-Donia, 2001

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of malathion, permethrin, DEET (N,N-diethyl-m-toluamide), and their metabolites in rat plasma and urine using high performance liquid chromatography
